message.content:
You are an adversarial cryptography reviewer. REFUTE the candidate finding below; default to refuted=true unless the evidence is solid. Read the actual cb-mpc code (root C:/bugBounty/targets/cb-mpc) before deciding.

CANDIDATE:
  title: Decryption-share DLEQ challenge omits the statement (R1, Qi, bases) and any domain separator from the Fiat-Shamir hash
  public API entry: include/cbmpc/api/tdh2.h: partial_decrypt (prover) / combine_additive,combine_ac (verifier)
  class: Weak Fiat-Shamir / missing transcript binding (NIZK)  severity: Info
  file: src/cbmpc/crypto/tdh2.cpp:89
  missing/incorrect check: The Chaum-Pedersen-style decryption-share proof computes ei = ro::hash_number(Xi, Yi, Zi) and verifies with the same hash, but the challenge does not bind the statement points R1 (ciphertext) and Qi (public share), the bases G/R1, a domain-separation tag, nor the ciphertext/label/session id. Contrast with zk_elgamal_com.cpp which hashes the full statement plus session_id.
  data flow: private_share_t::decrypt (api partial_decrypt): ei = hash_number(Xi,Yi,Zi); fi = si + x*ei -> partial_decryption_t::check_partial_decryption_helper (api combine_*): recompute Yi=fi*R1-ei*Xi, Zi=fi*G-ei*Qi, check ei == hash_number(Xi,Yi,Zi).
  attacker role: Malicious decryptor producing a partial-decryption proof for an honest combiner.
  impact: No concrete soundness break found: because the verifier recomputes Yi and Zi from R1 and Qi, a transcript only verifies for the exact (R1, Qi) it was produced against, so cross-statement replay/forgery does not succeed and Xi remains pinned to the genuine Qi. The omission is a hardening/spec-deviation concern (defense-in-depth, future-proofing against multi-statement grinding and proof reuse) rather than an exploitable vulnerability.
  excerpt: ei = ro::hash_number(Xi, Yi, Zi).mod(q);  // statement (R1, Qi, bases, label, sid) not bound

LENS = CONCRETE PROTOCOL-BOUNDARY ATTACK. Construct the precise malicious-party deviation through the public API that exploits this, and the concrete impact (what secret/forgery/DoS results). Honest party runs unmodified code. If you cannot construct a plausible attack consistent with the protocol, set refuted=true.

Set is_real=true only if the finding survives your lens against the real code. Return ONLY the structured object.

attachment.content:
- deep-research: Deep research harness — fan-out web searches, fetch sources, adversarially verify claims, synthesize a cited report. - When the user wants a deep, multi-source, fact-checked research report on any topic. BEFORE invoking, check if the question is specific enough to research directly — if underspecified (e.g., "what car to buy" without budget/use-case/region), ask 2-3 clarifying questions to narrow scope. Then pass the refined question as args, weaving the answers in.
- contribute:contribute: This skill should be used when the user asks to "contribute to open source", "find an issue to work on", "analyze a repo", "submit a pull request", "review PR feedback", "triage an issue", "sync my fork", "create a release", "clean up contribution branches", "debug CI failures", or mentions open-source contribution workflows. Provides a full-lifecycle open-source contribution system with 12 phases.
- update-config: Use this skill to configure the Claude Code harness via settings.json. Automated behaviors ("from now on when X", "each time X", "whenever X", "before/after X") require hooks configured in settings.json - the harness executes these, not Claude, so memory/preferences cannot fulfill them. Also use for: permissions ("allow X", "add permission", "move permission to"), env vars ("set X=Y"), hook troubleshooting, or any changes to settings.json/settings.local.json files. Examples: "allow npm commands", "add bq permission to global settings", "move permission to user settings", "set DEBUG=true", "when claude stops show X". For simple settings like theme/model, suggest the /config command.
- keybindings-help: Use when the user wants to customize keyboard shortcuts, rebind keys, add chord bindings, or modify ~/.claude/keybindings.json. Examples: "rebind ctrl+s", "add a chord shortcut", "change the submit key", "customize keybindings".
- verify: Verify that a code change actually does what it's supposed to by running the app and observing behavior. Use when asked to verify a PR, confirm a fix works, test a change manually, check that a feature works, or validate local changes before pushing.
- code-review: Review the current diff for correctness bugs and reuse/simplification/efficiency cleanups at the given effort level (low/medium: fewer, high-confidence findings; high→max: broader coverage, may include uncertain findings). Pass --comment to post findings as inline PR comments, or --fix to apply the findings to the working tree after the review.
- simplify: Review the changed code for reuse, simplification, efficiency, and altitude cleanups, then apply the fixes. Quality only — it does not hunt for bugs; use /code-review for that.
- fewer-permission-prompts: Scan your transcripts for common read-only Bash and MCP tool calls, then add a prioritized allowlist to project .claude/settings.json to reduce permission prompts.
- loop: Run a prompt or slash command on a recurring interval (e.g. /loop 5m /foo). Omit the interval to let the model self-pace. - When the user wants to set up a recurring task, poll for status, or run something repeatedly on an interval (e.g. "check the deploy every 5 minutes", "keep running /babysit-prs"). Do NOT invoke for one-off tasks.
- schedule: Create, update, list, or run scheduled cloud agents (routines) that execute on a cron schedule. - When the user wants to schedule a recurring cloud agent, set up automated tasks, create a cron job for Claude Code, or manage their scheduled agents/routines. Also use when the user wants a one-time scheduled run ("run this once at 3pm", "remind me to check X tomorrow").
- claude-api: Reference for the Claude API / Anthropic SDK — model ids, pricing, params, streaming, tool use, MCP, agents, caching, token counting, model migration.
TRIGGER — read BEFORE opening the target file; don't skip because it "looks like a one-liner" — whenever: the prompt names Claude/Anthropic in any form (Claude, Anthropic, Fable, Opus, Sonnet, Haiku, `anthropic`, `@anthropic-ai`, `claude-*`, `us.anthropic.*`, `[1m]`); the user asks about an LLM (pricing/model choice/limits/caching) — never answer from memory; OR the task is LLM-shaped with provider unstated (agent/MCP/tool-definition/multi-agent/RAG/LLM-judge/computer-use; generate/summarize/extract/classify/rewrite/converse over NL; debugging refusals/cutoffs/streaming/tool-calls/tokens).
SKIP only when another provider is being worked on (overrides all triggers): OpenAI/GPT/Gemini/Llama/Mistral/Cohere/Ollama named in the query; OR `grep -rE 'openai|langchain_openai|google.generativeai|genai|mistralai|cohere|ollama'` over the project hits (run this grep FIRST if no provider named — don't Read the file).
- run: Launch and drive this project's app to see a change working. Use when asked to run, start, or screenshot the app, or to confirm a change works in the real app (not just tests). First looks for a project skill that already covers launching the app; otherwise falls back to built-in patterns per project type (CLI, server, TUI, Electron, browser-driven, library).
- init: Initialize a new CLAUDE.md file with codebase documentation
- review: Review a pull request
- security-review: Complete a security review of the pending changes on the current branch

content.text: I'll examine the actual cb-mpc code to evaluate this finding.

The analysis matches. The verifier recomputes Yi=fi*R1-ei*Xi and Zi=fi*G-ei*Qi using the genuine R1 (from the verified ciphertext) and the genuine Qi (from the public-share set, indexed by rid). The challenge ei binds Xi, Yi, Zi. Since Yi and Zi are derived from R1 and Qi, the proof is effectively pinned to those bases/statement.

Key observations:
- The ciphertext is independently verified (`ciphertext.verify`) before partial decryptions are checked, so R1 is genuine.
- Qi comes from the combiner's trusted public-share set, not from the prover.
- A Chaum-Pedersen proof of equality of discrete logs (log_R1 Xi == log_G Qi) is sound here: a malicious decryptor must produce Xi = x*R1 with the same x as Qi = x*G, otherwise the proof fails. So Xi is forced to be the correct decryption share for the genuine secret x.

For a concrete attack the malicious decryptor would need to make the honest combiner accept a *wrong* Xi (to corrupt/manipulate the recovered V and hence the plaintext, or to forge a share). But soundness of the equality-of-DL proof prevents producing a verifying proof for any Xi != x*R1. The missing domain separator / session id matters only across multiple distinct protocol contexts where the *same* (Xi,Yi,Zi) triple could be replayed — but Yi,Zi already functionally depend on R1 and Qi, so any replay across a different ciphertext (different R1) or different share (different Qi) fails the recomputation. No cross-statement grinding advantage that yields a forgery.

This is a hardening/spec-deviation concern, not an exploitable boundary attack. The candidate's own impact statement concedes "No concrete soundness break found." I cannot construct a plausible malicious-party deviation that produces a secret leak, forgery, or DoS.

input.reason: Confirmed against src/cbmpc/crypto/tdh2.cpp:89 (prover) and :104-125 (verifier). The decryption-share proof is a Chaum-Pedersen equality-of-discrete-logs proof: ei=hash(Xi,Yi,Zi), fi=si+x*ei. The verifier recomputes Yi=fi*R1-ei*Xi and Zi=fi*G-ei*Qi from the GENUINE R1 (the ciphertext is independently verified via ciphertext.verify before any share is checked, so R1 is authentic) and the GENUINE Qi (taken from the combiner's trusted public-share set, indexed by rid, never from the prover). Although the Fiat-Shamir challenge omits R1, Qi, the bases, and a domain/session tag, Yi and Zi functionally encode those values, so any transcript verifies only against the exact (R1,Qi) it was produced for. Soundness of the equality-of-DL proof forces Xi=x*R1 with the same x as Qi=x*G; a malicious decryptor cannot produce a verifying proof for a wrong/forged share, cannot cause cross-ciphertext (different R1) or cross-share (different Qi) replay to succeed, and gains no grinding/forgery/DoS advantage. The candidate itself states no concrete soundness break exists. This is a defense-in-depth/spec-deviation hardening note, not a reachable protocol-boundary exploit.
input.attack_at_boundary: No exploitable attack at the public API boundary. A malicious decryptor calling partial_decrypt and feeding a crafted partial_decryption_t to combine_additive/combine cannot make an honest combiner accept any Xi other than x*R1, because the equality-of-DL proof recomputes Yi,Zi from the genuine R1 and trusted Qi. No secret leak, forgery, or DoS results.

content.text: Refuted. The DLEQ proof's verifier recomputes Yi and Zi from the authenticated R1 and trusted Qi, which functionally binds the proof to the statement despite the challenge hash not explicitly including them. No exploitable malicious-decryptor attack exists; the omission is a hardening concern only.
